name: Cambodia
tagline: Heir to Angkor and the Khmer Empire, scarred by genocide, reborn as a constitutional kingdom under the shadow of one family's long rule.
summary: Cambodia is the modern heir of one of Southeast Asia's most brilliant civilizations. From the Hinduized kingdoms of Funan and Chenla rose the Khmer Empire (802-1431), whose god-kings raised Angkor Wat and the Bayon and ruled much of mainland Southeast Asia before Thai and Vietnamese pressure forced the abandonment of Angkor. Reduced to a buffer state, Cambodia became a French protectorate in 1863 and part of French Indochina, regaining full independence under King Norodom Sihanouk on 9 November 1953. The country was then swept into the Vietnam War, endured the catastrophic Khmer Rouge genocide of 1975-79, a Vietnamese occupation, and a United Nations transition that restored the monarchy in 1993; it remains a kingdom dominated since the 1980s by Hun Sen and, since 2023, his son Hun Manet.

founding.label: Independence of the Kingdom of Cambodia from France
founding.detail: On 9 November 1953 King Norodom Sihanouk formally declared the full independence of Cambodia, completing the transfer of sovereignty from France after his self-styled Royal Crusade for Independence. Cambodia had been a French protectorate since 1863 and part of French Indochina from 1887; over 1953 the French handed back control of the police and judiciary in August and command of the army in October, and on 9 November General de Langlade ceremonially surrendered authority before the Royal Palace in Phnom Penh. The date is celebrated as Cambodia's Independence Day. This modern founding moment stands at the end of a far longer arc of Khmer statehood reaching back through the French protectorate, the long decline after Angkor, and the Khmer Empire founded by Jayavarman II in 802.

quickFacts.label: Khmer Empire
quickFacts.value: Founded by Jayavarman II in 802 CE; Angkor served as capital for some 600 years

quickFacts.label: Angkor Wat
quickFacts.value: Built under Suryavarman II in the early 12th century, the world's largest religious structure

quickFacts.label: French protectorate
quickFacts.value: Established 1863; joined to French Indochina in 1887

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Full independence from France declared 9 November 1953 under King Norodom Sihanouk

quickFacts.label: Khmer Rouge genocide
quickFacts.value: 1975-79: an estimated 1.5 to 3 million people died, around a quarter of the population

quickFacts.label: Restored monarchy
quickFacts.value: Constitutional monarchy restored under UN supervision in 1993

eras.title: Funan and Chenla: The Indianized Kingdoms
eras.standfirst: From the trading ports of the Mekong delta rose Funan, the first great Hinduized kingdom of Southeast Asia, succeeded by Chenla, whose princes laid the foundations of Khmer statehood.
body: The earliest important state in the region was Funan, which arose in the 1st century CE around the lower Mekong and along the coasts of what are now Cambodia and southern Vietnam. Funan was the first important Hinduized kingdom in Southeast Asia, prospering as a hub of maritime trade between India and China. Its rulers and elites absorbed Indian culture, adopting Sanskrit, Hindu cosmology, and the worship of Shiva, often represented by a stone lingam, while Buddhism also took root, beginning a coexistence of the two faiths that would endure in Cambodia for more than a thousand years.

Chinese accounts preserve a founding legend in which an Indian Brahman named Kaundinya reorganized the kingdom along Indian models, a story that reflects the depth of Indian cultural influence rather than literal events. Funan built a centralized state with a theoretically absolute ruler supported by agricultural surpluses, a priestly caste, and monumental construction. Archaeological evidence from sites such as the port of Oc Eo shows a sophisticated society engaged in long-distance exchange with India, the Mediterranean world, and China.

From the 6th century Funan was eclipsed by Chenla, a kingdom established in the middle Mekong region whose rulers were descended from the Funanese line and absorbed much of its domain. Chenla extended its power into present-day Laos and Thailand before fragmenting, in Chinese sources, into a Land Chenla in the interior and a Water Chenla nearer the coast late in the 8th century. This political disunity, and a period of subordination to maritime powers, set the stage for the unification that would create the Khmer Empire.

events.title: Rise of Funan
events.summary: Funan emerges around the Mekong delta as the first major Hinduized kingdom of Southeast Asia, prospering on maritime trade between India and China.

events.title: Chenla supplants Funan
events.summary: Chenla, ruled by princes descended from the kings of Funan, absorbs Funan's territory and becomes the dominant power in the region.

events.title: Fragmentation of Chenla
events.summary: Chenla splits into a Land Chenla and a Water Chenla and loses cohesion, leaving the Khmer lands divided on the eve of the Angkorian unification.

pullquote.text: Funan was the first important Hinduized kingdom in Southeast Asia, and from its institutions the Khmer world inherited its gods, its kingship, and its taste for monumental order.

eras.title: The Khmer Empire and Angkor
eras.standfirst: From 802 the god-kings of Angkor ruled much of mainland Southeast Asia, raising Angkor Wat and the Bayon in one of the most spectacular flowerings of premodern civilization.
body: The Khmer Empire is conventionally dated from 802, when the prince Jayavarman II, returning from exile in 'Java', defied foreign overlordship and had himself consecrated as devaraja, or god-king, and chakravartin, universal ruler, on the sacred hill of Mahendraparvata. Uniting the fractured Chenla principalities, he established a line of capitals north of the Tonle Sap that culminated in Angkor, which would serve as the empire's seat for some 600 years. The cult of the god-king, identifying the monarch with the Hindu god Shiva, became the spiritual foundation of the state and the engine of its prodigious temple-building.

The empire reached its height between the 11th and 13th centuries, sustained by an immense system of reservoirs and canals that watered intensive rice agriculture and supported one of the largest cities of the premodern world. Under Suryavarman II, who secured the throne by about 1113, the empire expanded across much of present-day Thailand, and the king began Angkor Wat, dedicated to Vishnu and still the largest religious structure on earth, completed only after his death. Khmer power extended west toward Pagan in Burma, south to the Gulf of Thailand, and east into the kingdom of Champa.

After a Cham invasion sacked Angkor in 1177, Jayavarman VII (reigned 1181-c. 1220) restored and expanded the empire to its greatest extent. Breaking with tradition, he embraced Mahayana Buddhism and rebuilt the capital as Angkor Thom, at whose center he raised the Bayon, its towers carved with serene colossal faces, around 1200. He also built hospitals, rest houses, and highways radiating into the provinces. After his reign the empire gradually weakened; the magnificent monuments of Angkor were inscribed by UNESCO on the World Heritage List in 1992 as the largest cultural site in Southeast Asia.

events.title: Jayavarman II founds the Khmer Empire
events.summary: Jayavarman II is consecrated as devaraja and universal ruler on Mahendraparvata, asserting Khmer independence and inaugurating the empire of Angkor.

events.title: Suryavarman II and Angkor Wat
events.summary: Suryavarman II reunites the empire and begins Angkor Wat, dedicated to Vishnu and the largest religious structure in the world, completed after his death.

events.title: Jayavarman VII rebuilds Angkor Thom and the Bayon
events.summary: After repelling a Cham invasion, Jayavarman VII embraces Mahayana Buddhism, expands the empire to its greatest extent, and builds Angkor Thom and the Bayon.

events.title: Thai capture of Angkor
events.summary: After repeated Tai attacks, Angkor is captured in 1431 and subsequently abandoned as a capital, marking the end of the imperial era.

figures.name: Jayavarman II
figures.blurb: Returning from exile, he united the Chenla principalities and in 802 had himself consecrated god-king on Mahendraparvata, founding the empire of Angkor and the cult of the devaraja that would sustain it for centuries.

figures.name: Suryavarman II
figures.blurb: A warlike and ambitious king who reunited the empire after decades of unrest, expanded its frontiers into present-day Thailand, and began Angkor Wat, the largest religious structure in the world.

figures.name: Jayavarman VII
figures.blurb: One of the most forceful kings of Angkor, he restored the empire after a Cham invasion, adopted Mahayana Buddhism, and built Angkor Thom, the Bayon, and a network of hospitals and highways across the realm.

pullquote.text: Stretching over some 400 square kilometres, Angkor contains the magnificent remains of the capitals of the Khmer Empire, including the famous Temple of Angkor Wat and, at Angkor Thom, the Bayon.

eras.title: Decline, Foreign Pressure, and the French Protectorate
eras.standfirst: Squeezed for centuries between expanding Siam and Vietnam, a shrunken Cambodia sought protection from France in 1863, becoming a colony within French Indochina until independence in 1953.
body: After Angkor was abandoned, the Khmer court moved south toward Phnom Penh and the kingdom entered a long period of decline. For roughly four centuries Cambodia was caught between two rising neighbours, the Thai kingdom to the west and the Vietnamese to the east, who repeatedly intervened in its succession disputes, annexed territory, and reduced its rulers to clients. By the mid-19th century the kingdom had lost much of its former domain and survival itself was in question, with both Bangkok and the Vietnamese court asserting overlordship.

Seeking a counterweight, King Norodom signed a treaty in 1863 placing Cambodia under French protection; France moved to check Siamese claims, and by 1867 Siam acknowledged the French protectorate. In 1887 Cambodia was incorporated into the federation of French Indochina alongside the Vietnamese territories and, later, Laos. French rule preserved the monarchy as a symbol while concentrating real power in colonial administrators; it built roads and rubber plantations and, significantly, sponsored the rediscovery, mapping, and restoration of the ruins of Angkor, which became a potent emblem of Khmer national identity.

During the Second World War Japan occupied Indochina while leaving the French administration largely in place, and in March 1945 Japan briefly ended French rule, prompting the young King Norodom Sihanouk to proclaim independence. France returned afterward, but the postwar tide ran against colonial empire. Sihanouk, who had taken direct control of the government in 1952, launched a Royal Crusade for Independence, touring foreign capitals to press his case. With France weakened by its war in Vietnam, control of the police, judiciary, and finally the army passed to Cambodia over 1953, culminating in the declaration of full independence on 9 November.

events.title: French protectorate established
events.summary: King Norodom signs a treaty placing Cambodia under French protection to fend off Siamese and Vietnamese pressure; Siam acknowledges the protectorate by 1867.

events.title: Cambodia joined to French Indochina
events.summary: Cambodia is incorporated into the colonial federation of French Indochina, its monarchy preserved as a figurehead while the French administer the country.

events.title: Japan ends French rule; Sihanouk proclaims independence
events.summary: Japan removes the French administration in the closing months of the war, and King Sihanouk briefly proclaims Cambodian independence before France returns.

events.title: Full independence declared
events.summary: After Sihanouk's Royal Crusade for Independence, France transfers sovereignty and King Norodom Sihanouk declares Cambodia's full independence in Phnom Penh.

figures.name: Norodom
figures.blurb: Facing Siamese and Vietnamese pressure, he signed the 1863 treaty that placed Cambodia under French protection, a step that preserved the kingdom's existence even as it surrendered its sovereignty.

pullquote.text: On 9 November 1953 the ceremony marking the withdrawal of French power took place before the Royal Palace, and command passed to the King.

eras.title: War, Revolution, and Genocide
eras.standfirst: Drawn into the Vietnam War despite Sihanouk's neutrality, Cambodia fell to the Khmer Rouge in 1975, whose radical revolution killed perhaps a quarter of the population before Vietnam intervened.
body: After independence Sihanouk abdicated the throne in 1955 to lead a mass political movement, the Sangkum, dominating Cambodian politics while trying to steer a neutral course through the Cold War. That neutrality could not survive the Vietnam War next door. North Vietnamese forces used Cambodian territory, and from the late 1960s the United States bombed the countryside. In March 1970, while Sihanouk was abroad, the National Assembly deposed him; General Lon Nol seized power, abolished the monarchy, proclaimed the Khmer Republic, and aligned with the United States and South Vietnam, plunging the country into civil war.

The bombing, the coup, and the spreading war drove peasants toward the Khmer Rouge, a radical communist movement led by Pol Pot. On 17 April 1975 the Khmer Rouge captured Phnom Penh and immediately ordered its inhabitants into the countryside. Renaming the country Democratic Kampuchea, the regime abolished money, markets, schools, and religion, and forced the entire population into rural collective labour in a bid to build an instant agrarian communism, sealing Cambodia off from the world.

The result was one of the 20th century's worst catastrophes. Between 1975 and 1979 an estimated 1.5 to 3 million people, roughly a quarter of Cambodia's population, died from execution, forced labour, starvation, and disease, with educated and urban Cambodians and ethnic minorities especially targeted. Relations with Vietnam, once the Khmer Rouge's patron, collapsed into open warfare. In December 1978 Vietnam launched a full-scale invasion, captured Phnom Penh in January 1979, and ended the genocide, installing a friendly government and beginning a decade-long occupation.

events.title: Lon Nol coup and the Khmer Republic
events.summary: While Sihanouk is abroad, the National Assembly removes him and Lon Nol takes power, abolishing the monarchy, proclaiming the Khmer Republic, and joining the war on the U.S. side.

events.title: Khmer Rouge capture Phnom Penh
events.summary: Pol Pot's Khmer Rouge seize the capital, empty the cities, rename the country Democratic Kampuchea, and abolish money, markets, religion, and private life.

events.title: The Cambodian genocide
events.summary: Under the Khmer Rouge an estimated 1.5 to 3 million people, about a quarter of the population, die from execution, forced labour, starvation, and disease.

events.title: Vietnamese intervention ends the regime
events.summary: Vietnam invades Cambodia, captures Phnom Penh in January 1979, ousts the Khmer Rouge, and ends the genocide, beginning a decade-long occupation.

figures.name: Pol Pot
figures.blurb: Born Saloth Sar, he led the Khmer Rouge to power in 1975 and presided over Democratic Kampuchea, whose radical agrarian revolution caused the deaths of perhaps a quarter of Cambodia's people before Vietnam ousted the regime in 1979.

figures.name: Lon Nol
figures.blurb: The general who led the 1970 coup against Sihanouk, abolished the monarchy, and ran the U.S.-aligned Khmer Republic until its collapse and the Khmer Rouge victory in April 1975.

pullquote.text: An estimated 1.5 million to 3 million people, approximately one quarter of Cambodia's population, died under the Khmer Rouge between 1975 and 1979.
pullquote.attribution: Terra Historica, on the Cambodian genocide

eras.title: Occupation, the UN Transition, and the Restored Kingdom
eras.standfirst: A decade of Vietnamese-backed rule and continued civil war gave way to the 1991 Paris Peace Agreements and a vast United Nations mission that supervised elections and restored the constitutional monarchy in 1993.
body: After 1979 the Vietnamese-installed People's Republic of Kampuchea governed most of the country, but the deposed Khmer Rouge and other resistance factions fought on from the Thai border, and the war became entangled in Cold War and regional rivalries. Cambodia remained impoverished, mined, and diplomatically isolated for much of the 1980s. Only as the Cold War wound down and Vietnam withdrew its troops did a negotiated settlement become possible.

On 23 October 1991 the warring parties signed the Paris Agreements on a Comprehensive Political Settlement of the Cambodia Conflict, which handed unprecedented authority to the United Nations. The resulting United Nations Transitional Authority in Cambodia (UNTAC), deployed from early 1992, was one of the largest and most ambitious peacekeeping operations ever mounted: it repatriated more than 300,000 refugees from Thailand and organized national elections held in May 1993, despite the Khmer Rouge's refusal to disarm and continued violence.

The 1993 elections produced a new constitution that restored Cambodia as a constitutional monarchy, and Norodom Sihanouk returned to the throne as king. An uneasy coalition shared power between the royalist FUNCINPEC party and the Cambodian People's Party, but tensions culminated in factional fighting in Phnom Penh in 1997 that left Hun Sen the dominant figure. The Khmer Rouge insurgency disintegrated in the late 1990s, and Pol Pot died in 1998, finally ending the movement that had devastated the country.

events.title: Paris Peace Agreements signed
events.summary: The Cambodian factions sign the Paris Agreements on a Comprehensive Political Settlement, ending the war and delegating transitional authority to the United Nations.

events.title: UNTAC mission
events.summary: The UN Transitional Authority in Cambodia repatriates over 300,000 refugees and organizes national elections, one of the largest peacekeeping operations of its era.

events.title: Constitutional monarchy restored
events.summary: UN-supervised elections produce a new constitution restoring the kingdom, and Norodom Sihanouk returns to the throne as constitutional monarch.

events.title: Death of Pol Pot and collapse of the Khmer Rouge
events.summary: The Khmer Rouge insurgency disintegrates and Pol Pot dies, ending the movement responsible for the genocide of the 1970s.

figures.name: Norodom Sihanouk
figures.blurb: Twice king of Cambodia (1941-55 and 1993-2004), he won the country's independence in 1953, dominated its politics for decades, survived the Khmer Rouge years in exile and captivity, and returned to the throne when the monarchy was restored in 1993.

pullquote.text: Under the Paris Agreements the Supreme National Council delegated to the United Nations all powers necessary to ensure the implementation of a comprehensive political settlement.
pullquote.attribution: United Nations, on the 1991 Paris Agreements

eras.title: Modern Cambodia: Hun Sen and Hun Manet
eras.standfirst: Stability and growth under the long, increasingly authoritarian rule of Hun Sen gave way in 2023 to a dynastic succession, as his son Hun Manet took the premiership of a one-party-dominated kingdom.
body: With peace finally secured, Cambodia rebuilt over the following decades into one of Asia's faster-growing economies, driven by garment manufacturing, tourism centred on Angkor, agriculture, and construction, and deepening ties with China. The country joined the Association of Southeast Asian Nations in 1999, and a UN-backed tribunal, the Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia, convicted several surviving Khmer Rouge leaders of crimes against humanity and genocide, providing a measure of accountability for the atrocities of the 1970s.

Politically, the period was defined by Hun Sen, who served as prime minister from 1985 to 2023 and built the Cambodian People's Party into a dominant force. His tenure brought stability and growth but also mounting authoritarianism: the main opposition party was dissolved by the courts ahead of the 2018 election, independent media were curtailed, and critics were prosecuted, leaving the CPP in near-total control of the National Assembly. Cambodia thus remained a constitutional monarchy in form while functioning in practice as a one-party-dominated state.

In July 2023 Hun Sen announced his retirement from the premiership in favour of his eldest son, Hun Manet, a West Point graduate and army commander, who was sworn in on 22 August 2023 in a carefully managed dynastic transition. Hun Sen remained the power behind the scenes as head of the ruling party and, from 2024, president of the Senate. As of 2026 Cambodia is a kingdom under King Norodom Sihamoni, governed by Hun Manet and the entrenched CPP, balancing close relations with China against the long shadows of Angkor's glory and the genocide that nearly destroyed the nation.

events.title: Cambodia joins ASEAN
events.summary: Cambodia becomes a member of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, marking its reintegration into regional and international affairs after decades of war and isolation.

events.title: Opposition dissolved, CPP entrenched
events.summary: With the main opposition party dissolved by the courts, Hun Sen's Cambodian People's Party wins every National Assembly seat, consolidating one-party dominance.

events.title: Khmer Rouge tribunal concludes
events.summary: The UN-backed Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia convicts senior Khmer Rouge leaders of crimes against humanity and genocide before concluding its work.

events.title: Hun Manet succeeds his father
events.summary: After 38 years in power, Hun Sen hands the premiership to his son Hun Manet, who is sworn in on 22 August 2023 in a dynastic succession.

figures.name: Hun Sen
figures.blurb: Prime minister of Cambodia from 1985 to 2023, a former Khmer Rouge cadre who defected, he built the Cambodian People's Party into a dominant force, presiding over growth and stability alongside deepening authoritarianism before handing power to his son.

figures.name: Hun Manet
figures.blurb: Eldest son of Hun Sen and a West Point graduate and army commander, he succeeded his father as prime minister in August 2023, continuing the family's and the Cambodian People's Party's hold on power.

pullquote.text: From the glory of Angkor to the abyss of the killing fields, Cambodia's modern stability rests on a single family's grip on power.

figures.name: Jayavarman VII
figures.blurb: One of the most powerful kings of the Khmer Empire, he restored Angkor after a Cham invasion, adopted Mahayana Buddhism, and built Angkor Thom and the Bayon, the temple whose serene stone faces remain an emblem of Cambodia.

figures.name: Norodom Sihanouk
figures.blurb: The defining figure of 20th-century Cambodia, he won independence from France in 1953, ruled as king and political leader, endured the Khmer Rouge era, and returned to reign as constitutional monarch from 1993.

figures.name: Hun Sen
figures.blurb: Prime minister from 1985 to 2023 and head of the ruling Cambodian People's Party, he shaped post-genocide Cambodia through decades of growth and increasingly authoritarian one-party rule before passing power to his son in 2023.

sources.label: Cambodia: World War II and its aftermath
Agreement on a Comprehensive Political Settlement of the Cambodia Conflict (Paris Agreement, 1991)
United Nations Transitional Authority in Cambodia (UNTAC)
